search_results.title: Kesavananda Bharati Sripadagalvaru & Ors vs State of Kerala & Anr
search_results.snippet: The basic structure doctrine holds that the Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution. This landmark judgment established that while Parliament has the power to amend the Constitution under Article 368, this power is not unlimited and cannot be used to destroy the basic framework of the Constitution.
search_results.bench: Sikri, J., Shelat, J., Grover, J., Hegde, J., Mukherjea, J., Khanna, J., Mathew, J., Ray, A.N., Palekar, J., Dwivedi, Y.V., Beg, M.H., Reddy, K.S.

search_results.title: Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India & Ors
search_results.snippet: The Supreme Court held that the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 is not restricted to mere animal existence. The procedure established by law must be fair, just, and reasonable, and not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. This judgment expanded the scope of Article 21 considerably.
search_results.bench: Beg, M.H., Bhagwati, P.N., Untwalia, N.L., Fazal Ali, S.M.

search_results.title: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, Architect of the Indian Constitution — I.C. Golaknath & Ors vs State of Punjab
search_results.snippet: In this historic judgment, the Supreme Court held that fundamental rights embodied in Part III of the Constitution cannot be abridged by Parliament through constitutional amendments under Article 368. The Court held that a constitutional amendment is a 'law' within the meaning of Article 13 and therefore subject to judicial review.
search_results.bench: Subba Rao, K., Wanchoo, K., Shah, J.C., Bachawat, R.S., Shelat, J.M.

11990.title: Kesavananda Bharati Sripadagalvaru & Ors vs State of Kerala & Anr
11990.bench: Sikri, J., Shelat, J., Grover, J., Hegde, J., Mukherjea, J., Khanna, J., Mathew, J., Ray, A.N., Palekar, J., Dwivedi, Y.V., Beg, M.H., Reddy, K.S.
11990.text:
This is a landmark judgment of the Supreme Court of India, delivered on 24th April 1973, which established the doctrine of basic structure of the Constitution.

The case was heard by the largest-ever bench of the Supreme Court, consisting of 13 judges. The judgment was delivered in a 7:6 majority.

The principal question before the Court was whether the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution under Article 368 was unlimited, or whether there were inherent limitations on this power.

The majority held that while Parliament has wide powers to amend the Constitution, it cannot alter the 'basic structure' or framework of the Constitution. This includes features such as the supremacy of the Constitution, the republican and democratic form of government, the secular character of the State, the separation of powers between the Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary, and the federal character of the Constitution.

The Court further held that the word 'amendment' in Article 368 presupposes that the old Constitution survives the amendment — it must remain the same Constitution, albeit in an amended form. If the amendment destroys the identity of the Constitution, it ceases to be an amendment and becomes a revolutionary act.

This judgment effectively overruled the earlier decision in Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967) to the extent that Parliament could amend fundamental rights, but introduced the limitation that the basic structure cannot be destroyed.

The doctrine of basic structure has since become one of the most significant contributions of Indian constitutional law to jurisprudence worldwide.
citations: Golaknath v. State of Punjab, AIR 1967 SC 1643
Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan, AIR 1965 SC 845
Shankari Prasad v. Union of India, AIR 1951 SC 458

157783.title: Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India & Ors
157783.bench: Beg, M.H., Bhagwati, P.N., Untwalia, N.L., Fazal Ali, S.M.
157783.text:
This is one of the most important judgments in the history of Indian constitutional law, delivered on 25th January 1978.

The petitioner, Maneka Gandhi, had her passport impounded by the Government of India under Section 10(3)(c) of the Passports Act, 1967. She was not given any reasons for this action. She challenged this order before the Supreme Court under Article 32 of the Constitution.

The Court examined the relationship between Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution and held that these fundamental rights are not watertight compartments but are interconnected and overlap each other.

The Court laid down the following principles:

1. The right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 is not confined to mere animal existence but includes the right to live with human dignity.

2. The procedure established by law under Article 21 must be fair, just, and reasonable, and not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. This introduced the concept of substantive due process into Indian constitutional law.

3. The principles of natural justice must be followed even when the statute does not expressly require them. The audi alteram partem rule (hear the other side) is an essential requirement of any fair procedure.

4. The freedom of movement and the freedom to go abroad are part of the personal liberty guaranteed under Article 21.

This judgment fundamentally transformed the jurisprudence of fundamental rights in India and laid the foundation for an expansive interpretation of Article 21 in subsequent cases.
citations: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras, AIR 1950 SC 27
R.C. Cooper v. Union of India, AIR 1970 SC 564
Satwant Singh Sawhney v. D. Ramarathnam, AIR 1967 SC 1836

94021.title: I.C. Golaknath & Ors vs State of Punjab
94021.bench: Subba Rao, K., Wanchoo, K., Shah, J.C., Bachawat, R.S., Shelat, J.M.
94021.text:
This is a landmark judgment delivered on 27th February 1967, in which the Supreme Court held by a narrow 6:5 majority that fundamental rights under Part III of the Constitution cannot be abridged or taken away by constitutional amendments under Article 368.

The petitioners, the Golaknath family, were barred from holding and cultivating their land by the Punjab Security and Land Tenures Act. They challenged the constitutional validity of the 17th Amendment, which had placed the Punjab Act in the Ninth Schedule, thereby shielding it from judicial review.

The majority, led by Chief Justice Subba Rao, held that:

1. A constitutional amendment is a 'law' within the meaning of Article 13(3) of the Constitution.

2. Parliament cannot amend or abridge fundamental rights through the procedure under Article 368.

3. The power to amend the Constitution is a legislative power, and any law made in exercise of this power must conform to the limitations in Article 13.

4. The doctrine of prospective overruling was applied for the first time in Indian constitutional jurisprudence, meaning that the decision would apply only to future amendments and not invalidate past amendments.

This judgment created a significant conflict between the judiciary and the legislature, ultimately leading to the 24th and 25th Constitutional Amendments and the Kesavananda Bharati case, which modified the Golaknath ruling by establishing the basic structure doctrine.
citations: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India, AIR 1951 SC 458
Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan, AIR 1965 SC 845
acts: Punjab Security and Land Tenures Act
